coredata.dc:title: Determination of dopamine based on its enhancement of gold-silver nanocluster fluorescence
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Ting
Su, Zhu
Tu, Yifeng
Yan, Jilin
coredata.dc:description: Dopamine (DA) is one of the most important neurotransmitters in human bodies and its sensitive detection remains a challenge. Herein, protein stabilized gold-silver nanoclusters (Au-AgNCs) were synthesized at first. It was found that the introduction of dopamine lead to a significant enhancement of the fluorescence from the nanoclusters, together with a red-shift of the peak. Through related spectroscopic and electrochemical studies, the fluorescence enhancement was attributed to the reduction of the nanoclusters by dopamine. This enhancement was then adopted for quantitative measurements, and linear responses toward dopamine in the ranges 0.01–1.7 μM and 1.7–10 μM were constructed. A limit of detection was obtained at 6.9 nM. The present study provided a facile and efficient method for the determination of dopamine, and the method was successfully applied for related measurements in serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Gold-silver nanoclusters
Fluorescence enhancement
Reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the BSA-stabilized gold-silver nanoclusters
Gold-silver nanoclusters and its fluorescence enhancement by dopamine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305100) and the Project of Scientific and Technologic Infrastructure of Suzhou (SZS201708).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence responses from the Au-AgNCs under 10 mM Tris-HCl buffers (pH 8) without (a) and with 10 μM dopamine (b). Picture was taken under UV lamp of 365 nm with the same experimental conditions. Au-AgNCs with or without dopamine were incubated at room temperature for 50 min. The fluorescence responses were collected with 370 nm excitation.

Fluorescence enhancement of Au-AgNCs with 10 μM dopamine under 10 mM Tris-HCl buffers with different pH values. Experiments were performed at room temperature for 50 min. The fluorescence responses were collected with 370 nm excitation.

Fluorescence enhancement with different lengths of incubation time. Experiments were performed with 10 μM dopamine under 10 mM Tris-HCl buffers at pH 8.5. All the fluorescence responses were obtained with 370 nm excitation.

(A) Fluorescence responses to dopamine samples at different concentrations (from a to m: 0, 0.01, 0.08, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 μM dopamine). (B) The plot of the fluorescence enhancement. Experiments were performed under Tris-HCl buffers (pH 8.5). Fluorescence measurements were conducted after 15 min incubations under 370 nm excitation.

Fluorescence extents obtained from different species. The concentration of dopamine and catechol was 1 μM, while that of glucose (GLU), glutathione (GSH), uric acid (UA), histidine (HIS), tryptophan (TRY), glycine (GLY), threonine (THR), Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Cl- were 50 μM, serine (SER), tyrosine (TYR), alanine (ALA), urea, phenol and resorcinol were 20 μM, ascorbic acid (AA) and cysteine (CYS) were 10 μM. Samples with Au-AgNCs were incubated under Tris-HCl buffers (pH 8.5) for 15 min. All fluorescence responses were obtained with 370 nm excitation.
Working principle of the dopamine detection.
Analytical performances of different detection schemes.

head.ce:title: Determination of dopamine based on its enhancement of gold-silver nanocluster fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Industrial Park, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Industrial Park, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine (DA) is one of the most important neurotransmitters in human bodies and its sensitive detection remains a challenge. Herein, protein stabilized gold-silver nanoclusters (Au-AgNCs) were synthesized at first. It was found that the introduction of dopamine lead to a significant enhancement of the fluorescence from the nanoclusters, together with a red-shift of the peak. Through related spectroscopic and electrochemical studies, the fluorescence enhancement was attributed to the reduction of the nanoclusters by dopamine. This enhancement was then adopted for quantitative measurements, and linear responses toward dopamine in the ranges 0.01–1.7 μM and 1.7–10 μM were constructed. A limit of detection was obtained at 6.9 nM. The present study provided a facile and efficient method for the determination of dopamine, and the method was successfully applied for related measurements in serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in human brains. It plays the central role in the brain's “reward” system, including the intermediary of pleasure, stimulation of significant messengers, or a false signal that promotes goal-related behaviors [1,2]. Our nervous system uses this compound to send messages between nerve cells and regulates various physiological functions [3]. Improper amount of dopamine relates to a variety of health issues. For example, patients suffered from Parkinson’s disease are found with a significant decrease of dopamine level. Besides, imbalanced dopamine level has also been affirmed to be a correlative of other neurological disorders such as Alzheimer's disease and Schizophrenia [4]. Dopamine has also been linked to various addictive behaviors and some profound social deficits [5]. Since the concentration of dopamine in vivo is rather low, sensitive and efficient assays are in urgent demand in related researches. In recent years, a variety of analytical approaches have been developed for the monitoring of dopamine, e.g. electrochemical [6,7], colorimetric [8], chemiluminescence [9] and fluorescence [10–13]. In these methods, electrochemical approaches are straightforward from the direct oxidation of dopamine. Unfortunately it usually suffers from interferences with a similar oxidation potential to dopamine, e.g. ascorbic acid (AA). Also, products from the detection (oxidation) also bring in pollution of the electrode and thus extra difficulties in the detection [14]. Colorimetric methods are more or less deficient in sensitivity. Fluorometric measurements have advantageous for the high sensitivity, and even better performances could be obtained with nano-sized fluorophores, such as carbon quantum dots [15] and graphene [16]. Recently, mononuclear noble metal nanoclusters such as gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and silver one (AgNCs) with different ligands have been applied to a wide range of targets, from inorganic ions, small molecules to large entities as biomacromolecules [17–21].

In this work, bimetallic gold-silver nanoclusters (Au-AgNCs) with a protein as the template were synthesized through a one-pot process. It was found that under suitable conditions, the fluorescence intensity of these Au-AgNCs increased with the addition of dopamine. Different from conventional quenching schemes [22–26], this response provided a facile turn-on signaling of dopamine. The mechanism of the detection was explained through electrochemical and spectroscopic measures. This gave a new strategy for trace detection down to nanomolar level. The method was then successfully applied to the determination of dopamine in related samples.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was from Aladdin Biochemical Reagent Co. (China). Dopamine was from Sigma-Aldrich (United States) and catechol was from J&K Chemicals Co. (China). Dialysis tubing with molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) 3500 was from Solarbio Life Science Co. (China). Human serum samples (mixed) were from Yuanye Biotechnology Co. (China). All other chemicals were of analytical or higher grade and used as received. All solutions used were prepared with deionized water.

Fluorescence signals were collected on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) configured with a xenon lamp as the excitation source. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were measured with a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PGeneral Instruments, China). The size and shape of the nanoclusters were recorded under a Tecnai G2-20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA). Cyclic voltammetric measurements were performed on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, China) configured with a glass carbon working electrode (diameter 5 mm), a platinum wire counter and a saturated calomel reference. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) spectra were obtained by ESCALAB 250 XI XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) with the binding energies calibrated using C 1s (284.8 eV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the BSA-stabilized gold-silver nanoclusters
ce:section.ce:para: Gold-silver hybrid nanoclusters were synthesized with a one-pot reduction following a previous report [20]. All glassware used was immersed in chromic acid cleaning solution over 24 h, and then thoroughly rinsed with water. Under vigorous stirring, an aliquot of HAuCl4 solution (4.0 mL, 4 mM) was added into a 50-mL round-bottom flask with 4.0 mL BSA solution (0.3 mM). 4.0 mL AgNO3 solution (1 mM) was then introduced into the mixture. In this process, the molar ratio of HAuCl4 and AgNO3 was kept at 4:1. After 5 min, 0.40 mL NaOH solution (1.0 M) was added drop by drop and the obtained mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. The rendered gold-silver nanoclusters were transferred into a centrifuge tube and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before further use.

The gold-silver nanocluster stock was first diluted 5-fold with water. In 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes, 100 μL dopamine samples at different concentrations were respectively mixed with 600 μL Tris-HCl buffer solutions (pH = 8.5). Then aliquots of 100 μL of the diluted Au-AgNCs were transferred into the tubes, and the mixtures were diluted to 1 mL with water. After 15 min incubation, the liquid inside was taken for fluorescence measurements. All detections were performed under room temperature.

Commercially available human serum samples were first centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min. After that, 1 mL of the up-layer was collected and diluted 50-fold with water. The resulted solution was taken and analyzed with the steps mentioned above for dopamine standards.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gold-silver nanoclusters and its fluorescence enhancement by dopamine
ce:section.ce:para: Noble metal nanoclusters have been intensively reported for various analytical purposes, especially fluorometric assays [17–21]. These clusters have ultra-small sizes close to the Fermi energy levels, and the transitions of their free electrons give them photoluminescence as molecular species [28]. In this work, hybrid nanoclusters containing both gold and silver were synthesized through a facile one-pot reaction. During this process, BSA served as the stabilizer as well as the reducing agent. It converted the trivalent gold and monovalent silver into atoms in the clusters. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image (Fig. S1) shows these NCs have diameter around 3 nm. In Fig. S2, the UV–vis absorption spectra gives a characteristic peak at 276 nm, mainly from the stabilizer BSA. In fluorescence scans, the NCs were mostly excited radiation at 328 nm and gave out two peaks with maxima at 440 nm (blue) and 580 nm (red) (Fig. S3). These NCs were capable of providing stable luminescence after a few weeks storage [27]. This kind of properties made the nanoclusters good candidates for fluorescence signaling.

As compared to regular decreased (light-off) signals, the increased (light-on) signals here was advantageous for its low background and usually lower limit of detection. There are several schemes that may lead to increased fluorescence, such as Aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) [32]. It has been reported that dopamine could bind to the silver nanoclusters wrapped by glutathione (GSH) through hydrogen bonding to bring the nanoclusters to aggregate [33]. At a pH lower than the pKa of –OH groups on the DA, hydrogen bonding interactions between neighboring − OH groups would overcome the inter-particle repulsive forces and draw nanoclusters distance to aggregate [34]. Here, various methods were applied to investigate the interactions between dopamine and the Au-AgNCs. First the UV–vis spectra of gold-silver nanoclusters and dopamine were compared. It was found that the spectrum of nanoclusters with 0.1 mmol·L-1 dopamine (Fig. S4c) was simply an overlap of those from dopamine (Fig. S4a) and the Au-AgNCs (Fig. S4b), no noticeable red-shift of the absorption peak was observed. This implied that there was no significant change of the particle size of the nanoclusters during the interaction. It was also confirmed with no significant change of the morphology in the TEM after introduction of dopamine. These excluded the possibility of aggregation induced enhancement. We also tried to remove the free ions (e.g. possibly AuCl4 - and Ag+) on the nanoclusters through dialyses (change the fresh buffer every 2 h, 6 h in total), the enhanced fluorescence from introduction of dopamine into nanoclusters remained the same (Fig. S5). These ions were not involved in the enhancement.

To further investigate the origin of the enhancement, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed on the nanoclusters before and after interaction with dopamine. The binding energy of Au 4f7/2 could be assigned to Au(0) (84.1 eV) and Au(I) (84.5 eV) (Fig. S6A). Meanwhile, the binding energy of Ag 3d5/2 in the NCs was 367.8 eV (Fig. S6C) [35]. The XPS results showed that there was a very weak Ag signal in the hybrid nanocluster, indicating that the Ag species was not deposited on the surface of the NCs, which was consistent with a previous report [36]. It has been proven that since the anti-galvanic reduction, Ag(0) and Au(I) can coexist in an equilibrium in small metal nano-entities (sizes < 10 nm), which is against the classic galvanic theory [37]. Accordingly, these alloyed Au-AgNCs consisted of Au(0), Ag(0) and a small amount of Au(I). After the addition of dopamine for 15 min reaction, the peak from Au(I) decreased while that from Au(0) increased (Fig. S6B), revealing the redox reaction between dopamine and monovalent gold. Since the fluorescence of the NCs was highly dependent on their surface status [18], this reduction might contribute to the enhancement. Some typical species with similar reductive properties, such as ascorbic acid, catechol, resorcinol, phenol were also tried. Similar responses were obtained with these species, but with much lower extents. Cyclic voltammetric scanning were performed to compare the reductivity. Under the same environment, dopamine was found more easily oxidized than other species (Fig. S7), which would contribute to its corresponding high enhancement of the nanocluster fluorescence.

During the interactions with dopamine, the changes of fluorescence provided versatile signals. The blue peak remained unchanged during the interaction, it was possible to adopt it as an internal reference for the construction of a ratiometric fluorescence detection (Fred/Fblue, where Fred and Fblue were the fluorescence intensities at two peaks respectively). The ratiometry seemed promising. Unfortunately we found it troublesome in applications onto practical samples. Some native proteins in samples (e.g. blood) gave rise of the fluorescence signal in the blue part and interfered the detection. Meanwhile, the red-shifts of the red peak (around 580 nm) provided another means for the calibration through wavelength change (Fig. S8). As the nanoclusters spectroscopic behavior was highly dependent on their surface status, this shift was possibly from the change of surface environment after interaction with dopamine [38]. The recording of the peak maxima was limited by the capacity of the instrument, and accurate reading of wavelength was difficult. Based on these considerations, the fluorescence enhancement of the red peak (F/F0, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities with and without dopamine respectively) was adopted for further quantifications.

For the sake of selectivity of the scheme, species with similar structures and possible interferences from human body fluid (e.g. serum and urine) were analyzed along with dopamine (Fig. 5 ). The developed systems provided only negligible responses over most species such as common ions, glucose, glutathione, uric acid, etc. Catechol, because of its similar structure to dopamine, did render some influence onto the Au-AgNCs fluorescence. The rest of the species (greater than or equal to 10-fold of dopamine) would influence slightly in practical applications.

The above performances made the method results, we applied the established detection scheme for dopamine measurement in human serum samples. The processed samples were taken and analyzed. Because dopamine mainly exists in brains and its serum level is rather low, standard additions were performed. Recoveries as 96.5% to 100.5% were obtained, indicating potential applications in practical samples (Table 2 ).

In summary, the present study demonstrates the BSA stabilized gold-silver nanoclusters as a highly sensitive fluorescent turn on probe for the detection of dopamine. The method was capable of detecting down to nanomolar level with 15 min. Enhanced red-shifted fluorescence was unexpectedly obtained with the introduction of dopamine, which might be helpful for future research. Due to the lower requirements for instruments, this method will be useful in cases where these expensive and complicated ones are not available.
Ting Zhou: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Zhu Su: Methodology, Investigation. Yifeng Tu: Conceptualization. Jilin Yan: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305100) and the Project of Scientific and Technologic Infrastructure of Suzhou (SZS201708).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119519.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
